subject: RE: Meeting and next steps
body:
Hi Sally
Thank you for all your efforts. I truly appreciate all you are doing.
I was also surprised at Edward. He was off on a totally different tangent. Grand visions are nice, but one has to stick to reality ~ our reality = Two idiots who were driving a bunch of disappointed investors with limited funds to a circus.
I think the Edward chapter is closed. $5M just for discovery, then $50M and then $250M? Without even a clear concept??

Regarding the Arab investor, he is stuck at $0.70 cents. He is also not willing to pay the interest on the c-notes. On the other hand, I am not willing to sign off on any agreements with Pinetti and Jennifer that transfers any liability onto me personally. Also, (although I may be wrong) but I don’t think the investors will trade their c-notes to shares at 70 cents and even if they do, not fast enough. So, let’s explore some other options that are practical and doable.

Option -1 = dissolve the company and everybody gets to write off whatever they can.
Option -2 is a spinout from the core technology as we don’t have the resources to hire rock stars who can sell to government (Municipalities) as the sales cycle is too long and everything has to go through RFPs which take forever. As I mentioned in one of my previous emails, the old proverb: “Build a better mouse trap and the world will beat a path down to your door” doesn’t hold water. You must advertise and market to the world before the world figures out the way to your door. Maybe you can explain that to Master Camera.
While our nearest competitor Passport Inc. has raised millions and is doing amazingly well, we don’t have their monetary or human resources to market Parkimon’s tech. That is our reality.

As such we are left with the 2nd option. A spinout from the core technology. We have two unique items in our IP portfolio that we can leverage– if we want to spin out with minimum funds.

(i) Precise location accuracy
(ii) Billing based on time, distance and speed.

We can do two spinouts from that. The first, a lot faster and cheaper and the other a much bigger play (Insurance). Let’s look at both.
Please take a look at the Orangecone 2.0 presentation attached. It is ready to go as is, but we have several competitors who have raised a lot of money for EXACTLY the same that Orangecone 1.0 does. We can differentiate by developing OC2.0. Take a look at the reasons starting from slide 8 onwards and especially slides 23 ~28. Check out the massive amounts these companies have raised. Here are a few more points to think about:
1. Our payment tech allows me to fast track development time
2. We can complete 2.0 in about 3~4 months with 5 engineers.
3. Once complete, we can start marketing it through social media = $10K to $20K /Month for a few months will get us the traction we need.
4. 6 months of engineer’s time (development and support) will cost $120K. Add 20k per month and that’s $240 for 6 months at which time we will have enough traction to go to the VCs and raise money OR do a Kickstarter campaign for crowdfunding.
5. The least amount of funds and the least amount of time.
6. With an All-in-One proposition (see OC slides) I am sure an A series will hugely benefit all the investors as we will not only be able to show traction but get a really good evaluation by showing the VCs the amount of money others have raised with just ONE value prop (please see presentation to understand what I am referring to).
7. Any one of these big names, especially Spothero or Turo or even Parkjockey with billions in their coffers can acquire us without us having to grow the company for years (as their revenues have stagnated and they will be looking for additional sources)

The other quick spinout (relatively speaking) is Usage Based Insurance (UBI) Pay-as-you-drive (PAYD) and Pay-How-You-Drive (PHYD). If you Google “usage-based insurance” or the above acronyms yourself, you’ll see that UBI is exploding and disrupting the industry. Please take some time out and go through the following links:

https://www.thebalance.com/auto-usage-based-insurance-4132461

https://www.forbes.com/sites/sarwantsingh/2017/02/24/the-future-of-car-insurance-digital-predictive-and-usage-based/#3e670b1e52fb

The objective of my concept is to disrupt the disrupters. My idea can be huge if done properly. We use our location accuracy and billing platform to build a platform for ALL insurance companies to bid for a driver’s ride in real-time in a reverse auction. With millions of cars on the road every second of the day- this has the potential to be massive. Even the perception of this is huge and can be easily sold to a VC.

We could even partner with Microsoft for this or even an insurance company can fund us if we offer them an exclusive for a year or even a percentage of the revenues from the transactions.

The concept /vision is to have an app in the driver’s hand akin to Uber where s/he can get multiple offers in a reverse auction way from various auto insurance companies for “micro events” like going to work, home, corner grocery store, mother-in-law, etc. Also, on a per hour/day/week basis (for folks who rent cars or borrow their brother’s-in-law vehicle or Uber drivers or delivery and fleet companies, etc. the sky is the limit when it comes to different segments).

Select, Tap and done! The reverse bidding (lowest price) plus the user experience and interface for the entire process of onboarding, getting bids, and in case of a mishap, the FNOL (First Notice of Loss) process will be the disruption (both sides of the market)
Like I said, the User experience is the key.
1. Driver inputs his destination and
2. Gets an instant quote (reverse auction) from several insurance companies.
3. Selects lowest and boom - done!

You will note that the above is quite like the Airbnb and Uber’s model. You want to go somewhere, and you get instant gratification as both apps provide you with what you require. Mode of transport from Uber and accommodation choices from Airbnb - instantly.

Since the objective is to have millions of people using it, there is no way an insurance company can have hundreds of staff standing by and looking up a driver’s profile, distance, location, road and weather conditions and the hundreds of other variables needed to bid in real-time. Millennials want instance gratification and as such the only way this could work, is through Artificial Intelligence and machine learning where the insurance company’s system (that we integrate into) automatically bids based on pre-defined rules. To do this, we will provide the system, console and APIs to all insurance companies willing to come on board our platform. (They will of course be able to configure their own ratings/algos, etc. based on their own policies)

Using my core engine, I could build this in about 6 ~9 months. Ideally it would be great if we have an insurance company as a partner AND who foots the bill for the development. Maybe if you could talk to the gentleman you know in Progressive? I would need one or two days max for discovery - not one year.
Please discuss the above three options with Paul and let me know if he can convince his buddies to come up with $250K for Option -2 (Orangecone 2.0) or not.

Sally I would like to close this chapter and move on with my life. I will be sending out a similar update to all the investors and give them all the above options. If I do not hear from them, (as mostly don’t bother) I’ll be dissolving the company as I am sure you’ll agree this has gone on too long.

If the investors decide to go with any of the above options, then following are my terms of engagement:

1. I step down as CEO and we find one to take over the role. He must be someone known and proven in the startup world.
2. A board of directors with full fiscal and operational authority except they cannot dilute me. (we are going to get diluted anyways as a good CEO will want equity - that has to come out from the current cap table)
3. All prior bills to be paid. That includes WSGR ($17K) and me. However, instead of the lump sum owed, I’ll take it in salary form on a monthly basis of $10k to pay off my loans while I slaved for Massah Camera.
4. A few other minor details that I am sure everyone will find very reasonable.

All the above is something that you, Don Paolo and the investors need to discuss seriously and decide on. Like I said, I will be sending out the investor update next week, I don’t hear from them for the next seven days and Parkimon is dissolved.
I would like your take on the above before I send out the update.
Once again, thank you!
Kind regards
Asad

subject: Meeting
body:
Asad,

I left the meeting yesterday quite stunned as Edward did a complete shift in our meeting that I was unprepared for. In our prior discussion we had discussed ways in which Parkimon could be re-positioned to a stronger market platform and we specially discussed in detail the shipping industry and the potential synergies which could provide a solution a current industry logistics problem. Edward in his time alone seemed to move the solution to a much higher level.

While I obviously couldn’t control the outcome and paid Edward for his expertise, which I continue to value - I can see it has not brought you any solutions - so my apologies are warranted.

While I do not have any other avenues to turn to at the moment, please know I will support you in whatever you need the investors to do to make the most of this offer you have going.

Regards,
Sally

subject: ~RE: Meeting and next steps
body: Hi Sally, The quickest way to review Progressive’s snapshot was to look at reviews of actual users using their app and services. The App stores had thousands of negative reviews. Here is the link. That prompted me to look at other insurance companies offering the same service and technology, so I looked up Metromile, Allstate, Nationwide, Esurance, Statefarm too. What’s really intriguing is that ALL of them seem to be having the same problems. For instance: • The app has to be on all of the time and kills battery. • The tech cannot differentiate between acceleration, fast acceleration a brake and a hard brake • It cannot tell if the user is a driver, running, riding a bike or a passenger. • None of the apps seem to be able to calculate the exact position and true mileage of the driver. • If you’re going over a dirt road with potholes and must hit the brake a number of times – you’re going to be recorded as a crazy driver and your premium is going to jump sky high. (Total reliance on bad tech) Here are just a few quotes (common among others) from real users of Progressive which will tell you why every insurance company offering this service is not gaining traction for the service: “It detects phone movement inside a cup holder as Hard Braking! 5 miles per hour while backing up from driveway and you stop, guess what?! HARD BRAKES!” “apparently listening to music on my phone is considered using my phone while driving which counts against you and anything but a granny stop is considered hard braking. The app is incredibly intrusive inaccurate and a constant pain in the ass for the small discount there willing to give you. having a clean driving record and no accidents or claims for 25 plus years should be more than enough!” “The logic behind how this app rates driving is insane. It will downgrade your driving for "hard braking" for using your brakes to stop at a yellow or red light or stopping to avoid the idiot in front of you who just slammed on their brakes. By downgrading your safe driving (not driving through dangerous yellow/red lights or rear ending the car in front of you) the "hints" seem to be telling you to run those lights or run into the idiot in front of you so you can get a discount.” Seems to me that all insurance companies have used a similar inside-out approach rather than an outside-in. They are selling the “perception” of usage-based insurance, but their algorithms for braking, acceleration, cornering, mileage, actual phone use, etc. that they base their quotes on are all wrong; which is messing with their adoption rate and churn is off the charts. Very surprising, as there are so many really smart engineers in the US. Technology is the easy part, the problem that I can see is that the insurance companies have a holistic idea of what UBI is and have jumped on the bandwagon. They are advertising jobs to develop these apps without knowing the actual skill needed to translate their business requirements to tech. AI and Deep learning skills, not machine learning. There is a solution to each one of their problems and therefore a massive opportunity but which will cost. As such I’m taking this off the table as an option. Firstly, I’m not interested in offering my algorithms to one company and also as far as Parkimon investors are concerned, although huge and tempting, it will cost more than what they can chew right now. Appreciate all you do. I’ll be sending the update this coming week. Kind regards Asad

subject: Re: Meeting and next steps
body:
Thank you for your email.

Let me think this over. In the meantime can you look into Progressive’s snapshot technology. I think there may be a play to enhance their product and open a new market for you. I’m pretty sure I can get us a meeting w executives but not sure your vision is the best fit - may need to refine it for them first.

Anyway I’d like to discuss a bit more after you have looked into their product and patents and then I can make that introduction.

Sally
Sent from my iPhone

subject: Re: ~RE: Meeting and next steps
body:
Thank you Asad - I do think the insurance play is a huge opportunity for someone but understand your reluctance.

Regarding your first email - I am confident you will get 100% participation in taking .70 cents versus dissolving the company and them getting nothing - anything is better than nothing. I guess the deeper question is - is the Arab deal a real option?

If it is not really an option, your OC2 is worth a shot, however, as you said there is competition and we have learned in our discovery that in order to get market share it will take offering discounts to potentially both sides of the transactions, despite having a better solution. I’m not sure where that cost plays into the funding equation.

Anyway, I know you are ready to move forward so we will await your email.

Regards,
Sally

Hi Sally, 
The quickest way to review Progressive’s snapshot was to look at reviews of actual users using their app and services. The App stores had thousands of negative reviews. Here is the link. That prompted me to look at other insurance companies offering the same service and technology, so I looked up Metromile, Allstate, Nationwide, Esurance, Statefarm too. 

What’s really intriguing is that ALL of them seem to be having the same problems. For instance:  

• The app has to be on all of the time and kills battery. 
• The tech cannot differentiate between acceleration, fast acceleration a brake and a hard brake 
• It cannot tell if the user is a driver, running, riding a bike or a passenger. 
• None of the apps seem to be able to calculate the exact position and true mileage of the driver. 
• If you’re going over a dirt road with potholes and must hit the brake a number of times – you’re going to be recorded as a crazy driver and your premium is going to jump sky high. (Total reliance on bad tech) 

Here are just a few quotes (common among others) from real users of Progressive which will tell you why every insurance company offering this service is not gaining traction for the service:  

“It detects phone movement inside a cup holder as Hard Braking! 5 miles per hour while backing up from driveway and you stop, guess what?! HARD BRAKES!” 

“apparently listening to music on my phone is considered using my phone while driving which counts against you and anything but a granny stop is considered hard braking. The app is incredibly intrusive inaccurate and a constant pain in the ass for the small discount there willing to give you. having a clean driving record and no accidents or claims for 25 plus years should be more than enough!” 

“The logic behind how this app rates driving is insane. It will downgrade your driving for "hard braking" for using your brakes to stop at a yellow or red light or stopping to avoid the idiot in front of you who just slammed on their brakes. By downgrading your safe driving (not driving through dangerous yellow/red lights or rear ending the car in front of you) the "hints" seem to be telling you to run those lights or run into the idiot in front of you so you can get a discount.” 

Seems to me that all insurance companies have used a similar inside-out approach rather than an outside-in. They are selling the “perception” of usage-based insurance, but their algorithms for braking, acceleration, cornering, mileage, actual phone use, etc. that they base their quotes on are all wrong; which is messing with their adoption rate and churn is off the charts. Very surprising, as there are so many really smart engineers in the US. Technology is the easy part, the problem that I can see is that the insurance companies have a holistic idea of what UBI is and have jumped on the bandwagon. They are advertising jobs to develop these apps without knowing the actual skill needed to translate their business requirements to tech. AI and Deep learning skills, not machine learning. There is a solution to each one of their problems and therefore a massive opportunity but which will cost. As such I’m taking this off the table as an option. Firstly, I’m not interested in offering my algorithms to one company and also as far as Parkimon investors are concerned, although huge and tempting, it will cost more than what they can chew right now. Appreciate all you do. I’ll be sending the update this coming week. Kind regards Asad

subject: !RE: ~RE: Meeting and next steps
body:
Hi Sally,
Our prior experience with the Arabs have taught us that nothing is real until the money is in the bank. That said, please do discuss this with Paul, Steve and Jennifer that if they decide not to convert their loan to shares, then the Arab is not going to take on a loan. I am also not willing to take on their loan personally for obvious reasons. If the company is dissolved their promissory notes won’t be worth anything anyways. Coming after me is not going to get them anything even if they win- which I doubt. For one I don’t have any assets and plus the notes were made out to Parkimon and Parkimon would be history by then.

Going forward, here is the short term plan that does not rely on the Arab and can be done in parallel. (as the Arab is more interested in Parkimon then OC and we don’t have to give him OC’s tech)

1. We raise just enough to complete OC2.0 and go for crowdfunding and/or VC funding.

2. In your mail below you have mentioned: “in order to get market share it will take offering discounts to potentially both sides of the transactions, despite having a better solution” I know it is difficult, especially with Paul hovering, but please get out of the revenues mindset. VC’s care about traction and growth more than revenues for the simple reason that you have to go back to them for more money and they get to get a bigger chunk of your company. They love A-B and C series as their investment and shares value is going up and up with each raise and rise in traction (their money being used for scaling and growth instead of revenues and sales). Another insight that I came across from a VC as to why they prefer traction over revenues is the example of Youtube. When Google acquired Youtube for $1.65Billon, they did not have a revenue model and uploading videos was FREE. As such Youtube had traction. According to a VC, and I quote. “We don’t care about revenues but traction because we know that 5 years down the road, some smart ass will figure out how to monetize the millions of users a startup has!” Very true in Youtube’s case as it now provides billions of advertising dollars to Google. Also very true in the case of Whatsapp acquired for $17Billion- that still doesn’t have a revenue stream but FB paid that much to them for their users.

3. Besides, the investors are not going to be getting royalty checks or whatever even IF we are making a lot of revenues. They ONLY benefit IF their c-notes convert to shares at a good evaluation. OC2 offers not just the perception but precedents of huge funding for EACH of the markets that we are aggregating into ONE app.
With the above in mind, here is what I propose:

A. The cost to take OC1 to OC2 will be 4 months @$20K a month for 5 engineers = $80K.
B. We launch the Beta in the 4th or 5th month. A couple of months before (3rd month), we start the social media campaign to start building interest and supporters. Add $10K for that from the 3rd month onwards.
C. Paul needs to figure out the maximum he can raise from the current investors, we minus that from the expenses and set that figure as our goal in Indiegogo and/or Kickstarter (who use an algorithm to push up those startups to the top of their page who get funded within the first 48 hours.)
D. So instead of the investors wiring the funds, they go on to Indiegogo or Kickstarter and “support” the company there within the first 48 hours. We get pushed up and raise a lot more money AND potential customers at the same time. (our hack of these two crowdfunding platforms). I’ve witnessed this first hand with the company I am consulting with. They used https://www.agency20.com/ and have raised $2.9M in less than 9 days when their ask was just $500,000. There are many other crowdfunding service providers who do all the marketing, create the buzz, videos and stuff for a fee or revenue share. It would be a good idea to go to them or even Agency20 for that matter.
E. OR we simply go to the VCs in a month or two of getting some traction through our social media campaign.

Following is a timeline of how much and when the money will be needed:

1st to 3rd month = $20k per month (for engineers) ~ Please do keep in mind that the ONLY reason we can finish the platform as big as this is because of the IP in Parkimon. An utter waste and shame if Paul or his buddies can’t figure this out.

3rd to 6th month = $30K per month (engineers and social media campaign)

By the 6th month we should have crowdfunded or received a term sheet from a VC.

If the current investors can come up with around $180k then the phoenix gets to rise from the ashes.

Let me have your thoughts.
Best
A

subject: FW: RE: RE: RE: synergies with texpo
body:
Steve, 
Forwarding you the email thread. 24 years of living in Dubai has taught me one thing. With the Arabs, 
nothing happens until it does. That’s not a racist comment -just my experience. The DOT POC is an 
example for all of us.  
BTW, I had talked to James our counsel at WSGR when you asked for an agreement earlier. He said it 
would not be needed as the c-notes or even your loan will only need to convert when both parties sign 
off on the term sheet. So rest assured, everyone’s and your notes are safe.  
You do however need to do some critical thinking re your lien on Orangecone- as other than dissolving 
the company, that will be the only viable option left if this guy doesn’t come through. If the company is 
dissolved, you’ll get the source code for OC. (you can even demand it now)  

Anyway, a few things to think about are:  What will you do with the platform? Can you run it by yourself 
as is? (this initial version has competitors with millions in their bank accounts). 2.0 can only be 
developed fast with the IP I assigned to Parkimon. Maybe you can think in terms of converting your loan 
to a c-note subject to us going to a VC and at that time – if you are willing. (I don’t know how well you 
know the rest of the investors but some who don’t know you might want some kind of an affidavit or 
something to get a comfort level that you won’t take off with OC when 2.0 is ready to go  ). I just 
don’t know. Lots to think about.  
Please feel free to call me if you have any questions. 
Best 
Asad

subject: RE: RE: RE: synergies with texpo
body: Sarfaraz While I can understand your logic, we are not at the drawing board stage and like I mentioned earlier, over 30000 hours have gone into R&D which can cost you over $7M if developed now. Also please do not disregard the fact that our POC with Abu Dhabi DOT was very successful and our LPR accuracy is over 99% for ALL GCC license plates. That alone is worth a lot. Regarding VC funding, I have not had the time as we were under due diligence with a Canadian firm who is interested in our tech. Localization will not be an issue as the UI is coded in XML. Also please note that the investor c-notes will only convert at the time your company closes on its investment. I will get back to you as soon as I hear from my investors as it is holiday season these days and most are traveling. Warm regards Asad

subject: RE: RE: RE: synergies with texpo
body:
Dear Asad
Your technology has not been deployed and proven itself commercially. I’m also not very clear as to why
you have not received institutional funding yet. A lot of work will need to done to customize and localize
the platform for this region. My offer is the best I can do. I’d like to make a decision ASAP as I have other
projects in the pipeline.
Kind Regards,
Sarfaraz

subject: RE: RE: synergies with texpo
body: Gentlemen, Thank you for taking time out. I’ll discus your offer with my investors, however, I would be grateful if you can reconsider the interest on the notes, for while I know it is forbidden in Islam, it is kosher here and the standard way of doing business. In fact, America is built on credit and interest. Also, 70 cents and no interest on top of that would be a hard sell besides being unfair to the investors. As such please reconsider one or the other. I’ll call you after conferring with my people. Regards Asad

subject: RE: synergies with texpo
body:
Dear Asad
Thank you very much for the email. Appologise for replying a bit late.
The product seem very interesting. We are forwarding the NDA to our legal for review. Once approved,
we will send you the signed copy soon.
The next step is that myself, Raheel and Zeeshan will be on Skype with you to understand it's
commercial viability.
Will be in touch soon.
Kind Regards,
Sarfaraz

subject: synergies with texpo
body: Dear Sarfraz, A pleasure talking to you and I appreciate your candor. As discussed, I’ve attached an NDA. Going forward, I’ve attached a brief presentation about our parking platform for municipalities which can also be used by universities and other entities like hospitals, buildings, commercial garages, hotels, etc. Very briefly, I raised $1.6Million from angel investors in Silicon Valley and completed the platform over a period of two years. (30,000 man-hours have gone into the development) We also completed a very successful POC with Abu Dhabi DOT and fine-tuned our mobile enforcement module. (The license plate recognition engine can read ALL GCC plates with 99% accuracy and without being biased has the best UX UI that I have seen anywhere) We are currently under due-diligence with a Canadian parking company who has made an M&A offer based on an earn-out that I am contemplating. However, if you gents think this has potential in the MENA area then I’d like to get an offer from you too. For the sake of transparency, I was also thinking of going to Silicon Valley VCs as startups with one tenth our technology have raised hundreds of millions of dollars. Spothero, Passport Inc, and Parkwhiz are a few examples. Our platform basically bills on time, distance and location and can be used for parking and tolling without any infrastructure in place. Once you have gone over the material attached and the links below, lets connect and see if there are any synergies. Parking and tolling is a massive industry and it’s a cashflow business with millions of transactions happening every minute of the day. I look forward to taking this forward. Please feel free to call me anytime if you have any questions. Best Asad

subject: RE: Sale of Orange Cone / Parkimon
body: Steve, You have mentioned in your email below: “just key facts point by point would help in clearing things up so we can all be on the same page.” Let me try and do that so that we don’t waste too much time on the history and timeline on the conference call. Paul please feel free to correct and or contend anything written below. 1. Seed funding for Parkimon was always done in a “drip-feed” fashion. 2. Paul’s job was to raise funds. My job was to deliver the technology and then execute – I did the first at one tenth the cost it would have taken and at the cost of losing my entire team of engineers (all 64 of them) as they never got paid on time. The second part was tough as you need a team to execute - which means funds - which we didn’t have. 3. Paul insisted we needed to show revenues so he could raise more funds and extend our runway. 4. Municipality /Government sales take too long to mature. (The Abu Dhabi DOT project is an example). I therefore came up with a consumer-to-consumer App (Orangecone) as I could build it very fast by using my payments and location tracking IP that I had assigned to Parkimon. 5. Orangecone was a spinout – The definition of a spinout is: “A spinout is a type of corporate realignment involving the separation of a division to form a new independent corporation”. Orangecone (OC) was thus incorporated as a separate C-Corp entity from Parkimon. - I just used my own IP to develop OC very quickly so we could get “revenues” as required by Paul and his investors. 6. However, Paul could not provide the funds to market and promote OC too. The right way would have been through social media like Parkwhiz (exact duplicate of Orangecone) did to become a very successful company. A complete social media plan of Parkwhiz was given to Paul and his cohorts. (See attached) However, due to limited funds, Paul expected me to go down to Santa Cruz, knock on doors and get the homeowners living near beaches to give us their driveways to rent on OC. 7. Also, when you’re working nights on India time which is 13 hours ahead, it is a little difficult to get up at the crack of dawn and go out and sell, especially if you’ve slept at the crack of dawn. (In case you are wondering why I had to code together with my engineers till the crack of our dawn, it was because (i) We could not afford the number of engineers needed, (ii) the core engineers who had worked on Parkimon were leaving in droves (because of not getting paid on time) and I had to train the new ones replacing them.) 8. Fast forward early 2019 and we were out of funds for the 14th time and desperate, as I was down to the last couple of my original team of 64 engineers. 9. Instead of finding further investment, Paul decided to get us a loan from Steve, Jennifer Ross and Thomas Chan to extend the runway. 10. Steve needed collateral for his loan and the only thing we had to offer was Orangecone (OC). The IP and source code were therefore pledged for the loan and since OC was a spinout/separate entity, the agreement for the loan and notes was signed off with OC INC. 11. We became insolvent yet again by June 2019. I took out a personal loan to keep the technology going as Gtechna, a Canadian parking technology provider was interested in acquiring our technology and wanted to test it. Although they liked our tech, they needed it to be in production with a municipality before they could make an offer for acquisition- Funds and time needed for business development – both of which we did not have. 12. The platform was finally mothballed by October at which time I was totally out of funds and friends. 13. I informed Paul that I had to dissolve the company as we were totally insolvent, and the technology mothballed. 14. From October 2019 to date, Paul, his friend Sally and I have been trying to find a way to resolve the situation somehow. 15. In November 2019, a company in Dubai became interested in acquiring Parkimon’s technology. However, they are not willing to pay full price (as the tech has never seen production) They have offered 70 cents on the dollar provided all the investors agree to it and convert their loans (C-notes) to stock at the time of acquisition. My conversations with the Arab have been going on since November. There are obviously no term sheets or anything yet because everything is up in the air as I have not been able to give the Arab a satisfactory answer as to whether all the investors will go for it or not. He is obviously exasperated and fed up and given a deadline which expires at the end of this month. 16. The reason I have not been able to give him an answer is because all this time, Paul and Sally were trying to find a better solution where they could raise more funds and revive Parkimon in some way or the other. The gentleman they were conferring with (Edward Strickland) wasted everyone’s time and bugged out just a couple of weeks ago. That’s when I sent out the investor update and gave the three options. (i) potential acquisition by the Arab (ii) Dissolving the company and (iii) Developing Orangecone 2.0 and taking it to VCs with a timeline of 6~8 months under new management, board and everything. (since as per the gospels of Paul, I can’t execute while sleep walking). 17. Gentlemen, it’s very hard for me to say this because quite frankly I rue the day I met this boy, but Paul’s insistence on revenues ~ which prompted me to develop OC1.0 could very well be the pivot and saving grace for all the investors and maybe a chance for everyone to benefit enormously rather than lose money on the Arab deal – provided that goes through. 18. The Arab is only interested in Parkimon’s tech for municipal parking and not in OC as there is no market for such an App in the Middle East. I have therefore kept him away from OC because of the following reasons. (i) Telling him about the convoluted reasons for OC and the lien would only confuse him not to mention show how desperate we were (ii) Since the agreement with Steve is with OC, a separate entity then Parkimon, Steve and his loan would not be invited into Parkimon’s bid of 70 cents and Steve would lose the opportunity to get back anything -even 70 cents on the dollar. (iii) I have a vested interest in making this work as I have my IP worth millions plus 3 years of my life invested in this. I hope the above timeline and background answered some of your questions. I can clear the rest up when we talk. James, please let us know the conference call number to call and the time. Best Asad

subject: Sale of Orange Cone / Parkimon
body:
Asad
Just had a confusing call with James that doesn’t match with our call yesterday.

I believe it is necessary to get you Paul, James and myself on a call tomorrow to sort out what is actually happening here and what exactly is for sale.
The fact that there is no term sheet is a bit alarming if there actually is a sale in the works.

I am happy to support what is need to get any deal done.
The deal points needs to be laid out neat and clean in a term sheet.
How the investors will be treated and funds dispersed also needs to be detailed point by point in writing.
No long emails or outlines just key facts point by point would help in clearing things up so we can all be on the same page.

Anyway, can we all talk tomorrow at 10am so we can keep this moving and not miss an opportunity that expires at the end of the month.

Steve
Steve Pinetti
steve@pinettiway.com
415 - 279 - 4319

subject: Re: follow up on parkimon investor update 2020
body:
You know I am in .70 on the dollar offer.
Steve Pinetti
Sent from my iPhone

Dear investors, Reference the update sent out on January 8th. where I informed you of the three options. (1) Sell your c-notes to the Dubai company that has offered to buy the c-notes at 70 cents on the dollar. (2) Parkimon pivots from government and municipalities to consumers, where we take Orangecone to Orangecone 2.0 by investing a little bit more (3) dissolve the company. Out of the 37 investors, I have received only 14 responses so far. 11 are for option 1, and 3 for option 2, while a few are still confused as to which way to go. Please keep in mind that for both options -1&2, I will need ALL 37 to concur on one or the other as according to our counsel, we cannot force any investor to take a loss nor invest further. Just so everyone is on the same page. I will need everyone (all 37 c-note holders) to concur and kindly respond ASAP if they wish to go for option 1. If they don’t, the opportunity goes away. Time is of the essence. Kindly do not delay. If you have any questions then please feel free to call me on my cell. Kind regards Asad Shah C> 415 515 6415

subject: parkimon investor update 2020
body: Dear investors, A happy new year to you all. I pray 2020 will be better than the ones gone by. I was advised to keep this update very brief, but out of respect for the trust you bestowed on me, I would like to give you some of the facts that have led to the plight we are in and why. Startups fail because of three main reasons. Bad technology, a bad team (execution) and lack of funds. Especially lack of funds, as both tech and team can be fixed and replaced with funds. We have excellent technology but never had the funds to build a team or execute. Since the sales cycle to municipalities is rather long and hiring someone to go after municipalities was not “affordable”; I was forced to come up with a “direct-to-consumer” App and focus on revenues instead. It was made clear to me that if I do not bring in immediate revenue there would be “no more funds!” Looks like someone misinterpreted the proverb “Build a better mousetrap, and the world will beat a path to your door.” Even word about the better mouse trap must be put out there so the world can figure out which door to knock on. The mousetrap must be promoted, marketed, sold and supported. But that too costs funds – funds that were not made available. Orangecone and then Stratosvue (direct-to-consumer apps) were borne out of desperation but the same old story unfolded. No funds to build a team, market, sell or support them too. Technology status - As you all are aware, our technology platform was piloted with the Abu Dhabi Department of Transport (DOT) and after the successful conclusion of the pilot, we fine-tuned it further from our on-the-ground experience there. 30025 hours went into the research and development of the platform and had we developed it in the US @$250/hr. instead of offshore in India, it would have cost us over $7M instead of less than $1.5M. The DOT pilot had gone so well that I was given the impression that we would get the contract. We had hung our hat on the contract, and it did not come through timely. The irony is that even if had won it, we would not have been able to support and manage it because of lack of funds. For that reason, we had partnered with a local UAE company, the Bin Dasmal Group. Anyway, due to organization re-structuring and management change at DOT, plus their sudden “need” for transparency, the contract was delayed and then recently publicly posted in an RFP by the government of Abu Dhabi; however, we could not bid on it as Parkimon has been insolvent and our UAE partner the Bin Dasmal Group had pulled out a while ago. Current situation - Since the last year and half, I have had to deal with funding drying up every month to the extent that Parkimon is now saddled with a loan of $400K. We’ve had a turnover of 64 engineers, when we only needed 15 because of them not getting paid on time. As of now Parkimon is insolvent and the technology has been mothballed. So, what are our options? (A) Dissolve the company, (B) Sell our IP (M&A) and (C) Find institutional Venture capital. Let’s evaluate each. A. Dissolve the company: The company has been insolvent since June 2019, but I was able to raise just enough to shelf the technology in such a way where it could be kickstarted quickly. Since Parkimon qualifies as a small business, dissolving the company would allow those who have not invested through their IRA’s to write off their entire amounts over time. Unfortunately, those who invested through their IRA’s will not be able to write off more than $3000 ~ I think. I’m not very clear about US tax laws as such please confer with your tax consultants about this. B. Sell our IP (M&A): There was interest to acquire our technology by a Canadian firm and one other but both offers are subject to our platform being commercially deployed and “proven in the field” in any one municipality. This obviously requires sales effort and the technology back off the shelf and live. Both of which require human and monetary resources. I have however, received an offer from a Dubai based company who is willing to offer $0.70 cents on the dollar for the convertible notes provided they have been converted to shares by the end of this month. Also, all loans to Parkimon to be converted to c-notes and then into shares of Parkimon, as the Arab is interested in buying the company, not taking on any loans. This offer is good till the end of January. As such I will need a response from 51% of the investors ASAP. C. Find institutional Venture capital: This is one option that is still available to us and can still benefit all the investors. Perhaps even more so then Parkimon. Please see the attached presentation of Orangecone, especially pages 21 ~27 and you will be able to better understand what I am talking about going forward. Although startups with one tenth our technology have raised hundreds of millions of dollars. Spothero, Passport Inc, and Parkwhiz are a few examples and as such validate Parkimon’s technology. VC’s don’t really like investing in startups that rely on government /municipal contracts. As you can imagine, government contracts take too long to mature, that is, the sales cycle is too long. VC’s care more about traction and growth over revenues for the simple reason that you have to go back to them for more money and they get a bigger chunk of your company. They love A-B and C series as their investment and stock value is going up with each raise and rise in traction (their money is being used for scaling and growth instead of revenues and sales). And this benefits early investors too as their C-notes convert to a higher valuation (provided there is traction (users)). Another insight that I came across from a VC as to why they prefer traction over revenues is the example of YouTube. When Google acquired YouTube for $1.65Billon, they did not have a revenue model and uploading videos was FREE. As such YouTube had a lot of users (traction). According to a VC, and I quote. “We don’t care about startup revenues but prefer traction - because we know that 5 years down the road, some smart ass will figure out how to monetize the millions of users a startup has.” Very true in YouTube’s case as it now provides billions of advertising dollars to Google. Also, very true in the case of WhatsApp acquired for $19 Billion by Facebook for WhatsApp’s millions of users. Keeping the above in view, I have a proposal that I would like to put forth to you all as an alternative to dissolving the company. We have in our portfolio a product (Orangecone) that is a pure consumer-to-consumer play and does not rely upon government or municipalities. Currently it is akin to Airbnb for driveways where folks living near stadiums, beaches and tourist spots can rent out their driveways for folks to park. The same back-end technology can be used for renting out cars, RVs, personal trucks, boats and bikes and as such just the front-end user interface plus minor tweaking of the back end will need to be developed. My payments and location tracking technology can fast-track this next or 2.0 version of Orangecone from 18+ months to less than 4 months. If you look at the attached PowerPoint, you’ll be surprised to see that startups with just one value prop have literally raised millions of dollars and some even hundreds of millions. Orangecone 2.0 will not only differentiate us from the competitors but offers not just perception but precedents of massive funding for EACH of the markets that we can aggregate into ONE app. It would be the ONE App for ALL thing’s mobility. Another few months of focused social media advertising and promotion would get us enough traction to get a very high valuation from any VC and benefit the existing investors enormously. I estimate the total cost to be around $180K including the promotion while the overall timeline to be around 6~8 months before we can go to a VC, if not earlier. We currently have 33 investors and if each contributes $5500, we should be able to rise again instead of dissolving the company. Even though the potential upside for you can be much higher than what a VC evaluation could have been for Parkimon, not everyone may want to participate and therein lies the opportunity for others to step in and not only increase their investment (gain more at the time of VC evaluation) but safeguard their current investment. However, before we can go this route, Orangecone is under lien with one of the investors who was kind enough to provide a loan of $300K to sustain us from January to June 2019. The lien holder will either need to convert the loan to a c-note or paid off. I leave it up to you all to decide how, as with new management and a board in place, I believe this is a very good opportunity to benefit much more than originally envisaged. As time is of the essence, I would be grateful if you kindly think this over and discuss it with whoever as soon as you can. If you have any questions, please feel free to call or email me anytime. Once again, thank you for all your support and patience. Warm regard Asad U. Shah – PhD. Founder & CEO Tel : +1 415 515 6415 Email> aus@parkimon.com LinkedIn > in/asadushah Web > http://www.parkimon.com

subject: thomas and 2019 taxes
body: Raj, Since your questions are relevant to the situation and the investors should know the answers also, I am taking the liberty of blind copying everyone. It’s time for FULL transparency! See my responses inline in blue.

subject: Fw: R~E: Thomas Chan
body: Hello Asad, Thank you for keeping us - your investors - apprised of ongoing negotiations. I truly appreciate your perseverance, drive, and refusal to "throw in the towel" in spite of the formidable obstacles including, I suspect, misunderstandings & perhaps even broken promises. I had a couple of thoughts regarding a potential solution to Thomas Chan and a question from an investor that I would like to share with you. I am NOT copying or blind copying anyone on this email - just a "me to you, you to me" correspondence. Do you seriously believe I care who you and Paul talk to or copy? All his and your lies and half-truths can be out in the open if the both of you have the guts to face me in front of ALL the investors where I can show them actual proof of what the both of you did. Conspiracies, blackmail and threats of bodily harm, I can go on and on.. a) Thomas C: To motivate Thomas to prepare 1099s and "clean up" the accounting records in anticipation of a due diligence, perhaps you/the investors could include Thomas's $15,000 loan in the 70% haircut category, with similar terms to Steve P and Jennifer R. I suspect this would give him the necessary motivation to do what we need him to do. First, I don’t need to motivate anyone. Thomas also has a c-note, that should be his motivation enough. Second, he is not taking my calls. Third, the total amount the Arab is putting in is $1,183,000, which is 70% of the total amount of $1,690,000 that came into Parkimon. That INCLUDES the loans from Steve, Jennifer AND Thomas. Do your math Mr. CPA. In short, Thomas was always included – so tell him to get the ball rolling. b) An investor asked me about the purchase price, namely the 70% haircut. The question posed to me was: "Raj, how do you know the offer is indeed 70%... did you see anything in writing? How do you know 100%, or 200% isn't being offered?" The insinuation was that someone may be pocketing the difference. I told the investor I hadn't a clue, but at this point I personally am willing to take 70% on a "no questions asked" basis once the money (in my case $28,000) is placed in an escrow account. I thought you should be aware that you may be faced with this and/or similar questions. How kind of you to give me a heads up and to substitute “Someone for Asad”. You disgust me. Here is food for thought: • Did you or anyone actually take the trouble to see the technology with their own eyes? Other than Mary, Colm and a couple of others, did anyone download the apps and see how they work? • Even if everyone had. I’m an engineer, how difficult would it be for me to make a copy of the entire platform, change the logos and colors, rearrange the user interface and sell it off to the Arab in Dubai only- After all the whole platform is cloud based right? • Why even bother trying to get the investors their money back? • Why not pocket the entire 70% or 100 or 200% (that you insinuate), dissolve the company and be on my merry way? • I seriously doubt if anyone (a) would be the wiser, and (b) even if they did, what would, or could they do about it? Sue me? I was justified in dissolving the company back in June 2019 -as it was insolvent then! Did you even think of that fact alone before you came up with your crap above?? • Think about the above before you ever insinuate or question me again. Unlike you both, I have integrity. c) Similar to "b" above, I was asked if I knew anything about the "Arab" purchaser and the logic behind a 70% haircut purchase price was determined? Again, I answered same as "b" above, but thought it would be helpful for you to know this. The company’s name is Texpo. (http://www.texpo.com/ ) They are Dubai based and into defense and tactical systems, satcom, smart government and IT infrastructure. I have introduced the chairman to James Huie our counsel. Now let’s get to the logic of the 70% haircut. It’s the same logic that Gtechna, the Canadian company that was evaluating us gave. Although we did a very successful pilot and our tech is flawless, we are not yet proven in a commercial deployment anywhere. (that is, not proven in a municipality). Had our Mr. VP Business Development taken time out from his son’s ball games and gone and done some business development instead of expecting this brown boy / one-man-band to do everything, I don’t think we’d be in this position. Had you not advised Mr. Camera to withhold funds, we might have been able to hire someone to go out and sell and deploy and get traction and get a better deal. I wouldn’t pay a dime unless I knew the tech was working somewhere in production. Would you? Did you now get the logic of the 70% haircut or would you like a shave too? d) Last question: Why do we need 100% investors to agree on option "a" (the 70% discount offer)? Also, you agree that none of the C-Note holders legal/IP rights would transfer until we have the money in an escrow account, correct? As you know better than I, there are a number of sophisticated investors who have a lot of business experience, hence these questions are, I believe, reasonable. Raj, let me ask you a stupid question. If you had the option of buying a company, would you prefer to buy it outright and clean or would you take on its baggage and debt too? Why on earth would you want to be answerable to anyone going forward? Keep in mind that when a convertible note converts to stock, the owner’s level of fiduciary duty goes up as ex c-note holders are now stockholders and “own” the company. Why would anyone – if they don’t have to, want to deal with people halfway across the world and especially people they don’t even know? The Arab wants to buy everything outright and be done with it once and for all. I hope the above satisfied your questions. If you have more, I would suggest you email them to investors@parkimon.com so that everyone can benefit. Raj, next time you have a question or thought, be a man and own them. I know that the questions posed in your email originated from your own deceitful mind. I have communication between you and Paul where you connived with him to withhold funds, wrote and dictated emails for Paul to send to me, insisted Parkimon pay your German friend, Michael Calana 8000 Euros for a website template that cost him $128, threatened to sue Parkimon and jeopardize everyone’s investment if I did not give you 6% equity, and for what? Finding a few missing periods and commas in our proposal to Abu Dhabi DOT? As the guy who I relied on to know his numbers and who posed as an experienced CPA, you overinflated our proposal to DOT by 3.2 Billion A MONTH! Which not only caused me extreme embarrassment in front of their Chairman but convinced DOT that we were out to defraud them. In case you’re wondering why we didn’t get the Abu Dhabi contract, you can stop wondering now! In conclusion Raj, if I sound a little upset and bitter, let me assure you that little would be an understatement when it comes to you and Paul and the consequences of your combined actions. Withholding funds cost my daughter her future. She had received a 100% scholarship into MIT but I could not even afford to pay for her food and lodging in the Boston area as I could not even withdraw the little salary I was supposed to get. Furthermore, it cost me and the company the loss of 64 full stack EXPERIENCED engineers (many of whom I called friends and who I had known for years) All of them left one by one because they were never paid on time and had families to support. It was I who had to face my daughter and my friends! Every time I recall those moments, the bile rises in my throat – so yes! Upset would be an understatement! @Paul Camera. David Waal, Michael Kraszulyac, Nahn Le…and now ME, the list is extensive of all the malicious & stupid people that have wronged poor, innocent & brilliant YOU. Unfortunately, time is of the essence so stop whining and get Thomas to do what’s right by all and fast! A With best wishes and keeping fingers crossed for all of us, Raj

subject: Fw: Thanks and Two Quick Follow-Up Questions (Non-urgent)
body:
Hi Asad,
Thank you for taking time to answer my/our questions. Per your suggestion and in the spirit of transparency, I am copying the Parkimon Investors on two quick follow up questions and a comment:

1) Who and how was the 70% discount determined? Why not 66%, or 80%? Was the 70% a discount that you and Texpo (the potential buyer) negotiated based upon the pros & cons?
2) As you emphasize the importance of transparency, will you share with us, your investors, the actual written offer (or email equivalent) from Texpo to you outlining the key terms and conditions related to their offer?

Comment: I have not spoken to or otherwise communicated with Thomas Chan in over 18 months. Hence I cannot help you or the investors here. Unless I am missing something, it seems the burden of ensuring proper books and records are prepared rests upon your shoulders.

Wishing you good luck and again expressing my thanks,
Raj

Raj,
Since your questions are relevant to the situation and the investors should know the answers also, I am taking the liberty of blind copying everyone. It’s time for FULL transparency!

See my responses inline in blue.

I have communication between you and Paul where you connived with him to withhold funds, wrote and dictated emails for Paul to send to me, insisted Parkimon pay your German friend, Michael Calana 8000 Euros for a website template that cost him $128, threatened to sue Parkimon and jeopardize everyone’s investment if I did not give you 6% equity, and for what? Finding a few missing periods and commas in our proposal to Abu Dhabi DOT? As the guy who I relied on to know his numbers and who posed as an experienced CPA, you overinflated our proposal to DOT by 3.2 Billion A MONTH! Which not only caused me extreme embarrassment in front of their Chairman but convinced DOT that we were out to defraud them. In case you’re wondering why we didn’t get the Abu Dhabi contract, you can stop wondering now! In conclusion Raj, if I sound a little upset and bitter, let me assure you that little would be an understatement when it comes to you and Paul and the consequences of your combined actions. Withholding funds cost my daughter her future. She had received a 100% scholarship into MIT but I could not even afford to pay for her food and lodging in the Boston area as I could not even withdraw the little salary I was supposed to get. Furthermore, it cost me and the company the loss of 64 full stack EXPERIENCED engineers (many of whom I called friends and who I had known for years) All of them left one by one because they were never paid on time and had families to support. It was I who had to face my daughter and my friends! Every time I recall those moments, the bile rises in my throat – so yes! Upset would be an understatement! @Paul Camera. David Waal, Michael Kraszulyac, Nahn Le…and now ME, the list is extensive of all the malicious & stupid people that have wronged poor, innocent & brilliant YOU. Unfortunately, time is of the essence so stop whining and get Thomas to do what’s right by all and fast! A With best wishes and keeping fingers crossed for all of us, Raj

Am 07.02.2018 um 08:37 schrieb Asad U. Shah <aus@parkimon.com>:

Dear Michael
Your email responses leave me speechless. I believe it’s time to bring in others and end this dilemma and as such I will try and summarize so that everyone reading this email thread going forward understands the background.

1.
You were recommended by Raj as the expert who would not only fix our website content but make the website far better aesthetically than what it was at the time.
2.
Unfortunately, you did not deliver on time, you did not deliver on the aesthetics and you did not deliver on the content.
3.
Your conceptual and optical revisions, layouts and mockups, etc. were simply not up to par and was involving a considerable amount of Paul and my time to review and “fix” them.
4.
As time was of the essence, I got the website AND the content done for free - and everyone who sees it loves it.
5.
Although you did not deliver on any front; as a courtesy to Raj, I was willing to compensate you a reasonable sum for your “efforts” and gave you an opportunity to be fair-minded.
6.
You were not fair and demanded the full amount for not delivering anything –(as is clear from the following emails.)
7.
In fact you have invoiced us over and above that we initially agreed upon to the tune of $934.
8.
What really astonished me was that in this invoice you have billed for spending 32 hours on “HTML Programming based on the navigation”? 32 hours? Can I ask you why? WordPress allows you to configure navigation very easily – click -click and you are done - and even if you were to code it yourself, all it takes is a one line change of CSS. Also, were you not using DIVI the visual page and website builder?
Michael, I may have graduated back in the time of dinosaurs, when Cobol, Fortran and RPG were the rage; but I have kept myself up-to-date on 90% of the programming languages out there now. I sit with my engineers and write the architecture and algorithms myself. Learning new languages is a passion and I do this for a living. I am not being conceited here - just letting you know that whatever I have said above and in my earlier email below is something that I have years of hands on experience with.
Given the situation where you have not delivered anything, I am under no obligation to pay you and now not even for your team member’s efforts as it seems that I am being billed for their lack of skills. Although I am not obligated nor prepared to pay personally, I am willing to defer this decision to all the investors by circulating this entire thread and leaving it in their hands to decide if I should or not.
@Paul, Raj, kindly scroll to the bottom of the page and go over this entire thread from bottom up. I will then leave it up to you both to decide whether we get a consensus from everyone or not.
In the end, I would like to I reiterate and remind all of you that I have the fiduciary duty and obligation to Parkimon’s investors to use corporate funds judicially and will not pay for services not delivered!
Regards

Asad U. Shah – PhD.
Founder & CEO
Tel : +1 415 515 6415
Email> aus@parkimon.com
LinkedIn > in/asadushah
Web > http://www.parkimon.com

subject: RE: parkimon update
body: Dear Michael, I was quite disappointed from your email response. I don’t expect anything less from professionals other than “personal commitment and passion”, because that is what I always bring to the table in everything I do - at no additional charge. I requested you to be fair. I’m dismayed by the fact that you were not. Under the circumstances, here is my take on the matter. You did not deliver on time, you did not deliver on the aesthetics and you did not deliver on the “expertise” you were initially recommended for, i.e. fixing the content. Basically, you simply did not deliver per expectations and objectives. 1. You did not deliver timely • I needed the website up and ready for my trip to Dubai- You were informed well in advance of the importance of that. You were not able to deliver prior to my leaving. 2. You did not deliver on the aesthetics • Selection of weak images. • Not being able to figure out what should go in the main header • Navigation and UX glitches • Site responsiveness complications on handhelds • Other issues -Please refer to my emails dated Fri 11/17/2017 1:07 AM and Tue 11/21/2017 12:55 PM and Thu 11/23/2017 3:11 PM 3. You did not deliver on the “expertise” you were initially recommended for, such as fixing the content. • Besides the weak content there are areas where the content has been lifted from other websites. This is professionally unacceptable. We were contracting for your innovation and creativity. I can go on but suffice to say that your deliverables were sub-par and required lots of Paul’s and my time in seeking revisions again and again. Going forward, Michael, you have alluded to and unnecessarily forwarded private conversations between Raj, Paul and you and from the email thread it is clear, that Paul wanted to “pay you in full” because he feels “obligated” to Raj; while you are leveraging your personal relationship with Raj to get paid for non-deliverables -which is unprofessional and setting Raj in a very awkward position. In a VC or investor due-diligence about funds spent by Parkimon, how am I going to justify spending $8000 with nothing to show for? Raj knows most of the investors personally and if they drill me about it, I will need to refer them to Raj as he referred you in good faith as an experienced professional with high expertise. As a startup, Parkimon is financially constrained and as CEO, it’s my responsibility to be fiscally responsible. In the same token, as the managing director of Sparks, I am sure you would not want Sparks to be associated with anyone who begrudges you for being responsible and NOT squandering company funds. Given the high quote of $8000, (which is way above industry standards for a mere website); I expected a high-end, hand-coded engineered website. I was unpleasantly surprised when I find out you are using a WYSIWYG WordPress theme that not only allows you to drag-and-drop and build pages visually but costs less than $250, and is an “out-of-the-box” page builder that has many known anomalies, bugs and problems associated with it. Opening/loading time-out issues, continuous loading at the back-end by DIVI, memory issues, access problems to page builder library… The list goes on. Of course, these can be fixed, but why would I want or need to? In addition, using DIVI going forward would have locked us to it forever and if we had ever wanted to upgrade or switch, we’d be swimming in a sea of useless shortcode that would require additional resources, time and cost to fix. Were you not aware of these facts about DIVI? In reviewing your work list: 1. You are billing us for writing emails to us? 2. You are billing us for quoting /making an offer to us? 3. Given the skillset required for a visual page builder, your exorbitant rate of $110 /hour is switched to $130/hour to generate a quote for us? and do research? Who changes their rates midstream and why? 4. You spend 6 hours creating content that was cut-pasted from another website and to create a drag-and-drop blog form? I can go on, but I will not. As the head of Sparks, the onus to fulfill the client’s requirements is your responsibility. Given the fact that you did not deliver on any front, I do not have any obligation to pay you. However, since your team was not at fault and did what they were told by you, I will consider paying them for the hours and work that you have plugged into the work list. Under the circumstances, the following are your options: 1. Do not bill us for anything -which would be the right and fair thing to do as nothing was delivered according to our expectations and objectives. 2. Revise your invoice reflecting an acceptable charge for your team’s efforts. In conclusion, you must have noted that I did not copy anyone to save embarrassment. However, it is your prerogative to share it if you want to. If it was me, I would write an email saying that you have had time to rethink and since we did not get anything, you will not be charging. That is what I would do. Your call. I reiterate, as CEO, I have the fiduciary duty and obligation to Parkimon’s investors to use corporate funds judicially and will not pay for services not delivered. Regards Asad U. Shah – PhD. Founder & CEO Tel : +1 415 515 6415 Email> aus@parkimon.com LinkedIn > in/asadushah Web > http://www.parkimon.com

subject: Re: *RE: --RE: parkimon update
body:
Hello Assad -

Please have a look at the attached list of working hours. The costs of the photographic material are not charged. After an internal conversation with my partner, we find no reason for a reduction.

For me personally, the way the project went is extremely disappointing. I have invested a lot of personal commitment and passion but of course, we don’t want to leave you standing empty-handed. I will rearrange the page and you can use it as a Parkimon Blog if you want to. You will get a data package with all used content and pictures. The accompanying CMS based on Wordpress is highly developed and easy to use. The package can easily be installed on any webserver and I think you can do a lot with it.

I am sorry that we cannot make you a better offer and I hope that we will perhaps find a basis for cooperation in the future.

I am privately interested in an investment at Parkimon. I think your idea is very good and I see a great future for the company. Please send me the information for the general conditions.

Warm regards
Michael

I’m sorry for the delay, but Paul asked me not to negotiate with you. "Please do not respond until I have spoken with Raj later today. I am going to see Asad in a few hours and have dinner with him. I will take care of it. You will be paid in full…"

Anyway, we'll make you a fair offer tonight.

Best regards
Michael

subject: parkimon update
body: Hello Michael, A belated happy new year to you. Apologize for the disappearing act but I just got back a week ago and have been a little overwhelmed. Quick update; while in Dubai I hired a local who is an engineer working for Microsoft to handle our demos and stuff there. Besides being an engineer, she also freelances as a designer. Anyway, she did our entire current website AND the content for free. We all love it and I am sure you will too. Michael, when Raj introduced you, I was facing content issues and did not have time to write the content. I have an entire team of engineers in India but my rationale was that there are very nice design templates out there that I could use, but getting the content right was my main concern. Raj said, you would be able to not only create the right content but do the designing too. Anyway, it did not work out. However, you put in a lot of effort and I respect that and you should be compensated for that effort. Please do let me know what would be a fair amount? I hold you and Raj in high esteem and am sure that there will be opportunities for something mutually beneficial later. I look forward to your response. Warm regards Asad

subject: RE: ##RE: ~FW: Re: final invoice
body:
Dear Michael,
1. The email that you are referring to below was sent on Thursday, February 1, 2018 11:17 PM and not on the 2nd and was sent after I had written you a long email and raised my concerns (none of which you responded to) It not only allowed you to save face but re-consider and give me a “fair” number.
Paul Dec. 1
2. Paul’s comments. Not mine.
3. Regarding a version of http://www.innobaytsolutions.com website. Please note that I loved their website and as time was of the essence, I requested Shamlia to use her own template. The fact that it clearly shows “Designed By www.innobaytsolutions.com” at the bottom of our page is proof that we did not ourselves publish a replica but asked their permission to use it.
4. Regarding expectations:
i.
I did not expect to be billed for writing emails to us
ii.
I did not expect to be billed for quoting us
iii.
I did not expect to be billed for 32 hours of HTML programming that was not needed
iv.
I did not expect you to charge me $8934 for a $250 drag and drop visual page builder with known problems.
v.
I did not expect you to lock me into a framework that would require us to be locked to you for any minor updates going forward and getting billed for those.
Michael, I had a lot of expectations, but most of all I expected you to be fair.
This rhetoric is over as far as I am concerned.

I am sorry that we cannot make you a better offer and I hope that we will perhaps find a basis for cooperation in the future.

I am privately interested in an investment at Parkimon. I think your idea is very good and I see a great future for the company. Please send me the information for the general conditions.

Warm regards
Michael

Dear Asad -

I’m sorry for the delay, but Paul asked me not to negotiate with you. "Please do not respond until I have spoken with Raj later today. I am going to see Asad in a few hours and have dinner with him. I will take care of it. You will be paid in full…"

Anyway, we'll make you a fair offer tonight.

Best regards
Michael

subject: parkimon update
body:
Hello Michael,
A belated happy new year to you.
Apologize for the disappearing act but I just got back a week ago and have been a little overwhelmed.
Quick update; while in Dubai I hired a local who is an engineer working for Microsoft to handle our demos and stuff there. Besides being an engineer, she also freelances as a designer. Anyway, she did our entire current website AND the content for free. We all love it and I am sure you will too.
Michael, when Raj introduced you, I was facing content issues and did not have time to write the content. I have an entire team of engineers in India but my rationale was that there are very nice design templates out there that I could use, but getting the content right was my main concern. Raj said, you would be able to not only create the right content but do the designing too. Anyway, it did not work out. However, you put in a lot of effort and I respect that and you should be compensated for that effort. Please do let me know what would be a fair amount? I hold you and Raj in high esteem and am sure that there will be opportunities for something mutually beneficial later.
I look forward to your response.
Warm regards
Asad

subject: Interim Board
body: Dear Asad: An interim board has been put in place and minutes from our first meeting will be sent to you shortly. Your resignation of June 12, 2020 as CEO has been acknowledged and accepted by the interim board. Whether or not you can reappoint yourself CEO, after the fact, is subject to debate but for the time being the interim board is not going to make issue of it. The interim board, however, will remain intact and provide requisite investor representation with the 3 largest investors in the company comprising a quorum, along with Paul. If you would like to come on to the board you are welcome to do so and will be given notification of our next meeting Investor Correspondence: Going forward investors will be apprised of all material items by the board, and no one individually will contact investors without the mutual; consent of the board. Many of the investors have asked you, directly, to stop this practice, and many have asked me, to ask you to stop. I, too, have asked you directly, numerous times, to discontinue this practice, yet you persist. Please, Asad, respect what I am asking of you and respect the wishes of the interim board as well as the investors. If there is need for the investors to be apprised of any particular item or items, the board will make that determination and we will reach out to them as a collective. You continue to state you want what is best for the investors. Well, that time is upon us right now with James Cutler. He has been working on trying to get some movement with and for us for the past 6 months on his own dime – we are on calls with him every week. It sure seems to me and others that he sees clear value in our IP and is not giving up trying to work something out for us all……. Now is the time for you to stand up for the investors and cooperate with James so he can truly evaluate for himself our IP. We do not really understand why this would be an issue for you especially after his signing your NDA. If you want to be paid a lump sum of $135,000, the interim board is amenable to meeting this request. This payout would assume you meet various benchmarks and performance measures, that would not be unreasonable. However, it's confounding to the board, given your time and effort put into the enterprise and makes little sense to any of us, particularly given the company's new found prospects. We would think that you would want to stay on and benefit with the group. Either way, the board and the investors expect you to honor your fiduciary obligation to the company and cooperate with Mr. Cutler and provide him with all that he asks of you, including making the software/source code available for evaluation. We trust you will fully cooperate. We have a bona fide opportunity here to use YOUR platform as YOU intended as well as a potential opportunity to procure some degree of potential funding. Additionally, Cutler is planning on a paid position for you and a programmer going forward. In closing I will be sending a very brief update to the investors next week and apprise them of the interim board's formation, next steps forward with Cutler, and the fact that we will hopefully have your full cooperation in this evaluation process. Sincerely Steve

subject: R~E: Interim Board
body:
Gentlemen,
Forming an interim board behind my back was quite inappropriate. I do not recognize this interim board as I am still the majority stockholder, sole Board Chairman, Director and CEO of the company. All actions that this board takes are invalid.
Best
Asad
